question:
A \emph{repunit} is a positive integer whose digits in base 10 are
all ones. Find all polynomials $f$ with real coefficients such that if
$n$ is a repunit, then so is $f(n)$.
solution:
Note that $n$ is a repunit if and only if $9n+1 = 10^m$ for some power of
10 greater than 1. Consequently, if we put
\[
g(n) = 9f\left( \frac{n-1}{9} \right) + 1,
\]
then $f$ takes repunits to repunits if and only if $g$ takes powers of 10
greater than 1 to powers of 10 greater than 1. We will show that the only
such functions $g$ are those of the form $g(n) = 10^c n^d$ for $d \geq 0$,
$c \geq 1-d$ (all of which clearly work),
which will mean that the desired polynomials $f$ are those of the form
\[
f(n) = \frac{1}{9}(10^c (9n+1)^d - 1)
\]
for the same $c,d$.

It is convenient to allow ``powers of 10'' to be of the form
$10^k$ for any integer $k$. With this convention, it suffices to check that
the polynomials $g$ taking powers of 10 greater than 1 to powers of 10
are of the form $10^c n^d$ for any integers $c,d$ with $d \geq 0$.

\textbf{First solution:}
Suppose that the leading term of $g(x)$ is $ax^d$, and note that
$a>0$. As $x \to \infty$, we have $g(x)/x^d \to a$; however, for $x$
a power of 10 greater than 1, $g(x)/x^d$ is a power of 10.
The set of powers of 10 has no positive limit point, so $g(x)/x^d$
must be equal to $a$ for $x = 10^k$ with $k$ sufficiently large,
and we must have $a = 10^c$ for some $c$.
The polynomial $g(x) - 10^c x^d$ has infinitely many roots, so
must be identically zero.

\textbf{Second solution:}
We proceed by induction on $d = \deg(g)$.
If $d=0$, we have $g(n) = 10^c$ for some $c$. Otherwise,
$g$ has rational coefficients by Lagrange's interpolation formula (this
applies to any polynomial of degree $d$ taking at least $d+1$
different rational numbers to rational numbers), so $g(0) = t$ is rational.
Moreover, $g$ takes each value only finitely many times, so the sequence
$g(10^0), g(10^1), \dots$ includes arbitrarily large powers of 10.
Suppose that $t \neq 0$; then we can choose a positive integer $h$ such that
the numerator of $t$ is not divisible by $10^h$.
But for $c$ large enough, $g(10^c) - t$ has numerator divisible by
$10^b$ for some $b>h$, contradiction.

Consequently, $t=0$, and we may apply the induction hypothesis to $g(n)/n$
to deduce the claim.

\textbf{Remark:} The second solution amounts to the fact that $g$, being
a polynomial with rational coefficients, is continuous for the $2$-adic
and $5$-adic topologies on $\mathbb{Q}$. By contrast, the first solution
uses the ``$\infty$-adic'' topology, i.e., the usual real topology.

descriptive_long.question: A repunit is a positive integer whose digits in base 10 are all ones. Find all polynomials $polyfun$ with real coefficients such that if $repunit$ is a repunit, then so is $polyfun(repunit)$.
descriptive_long.solution:
Note that $repunit$ is a repunit if and only if $9repunit+1 = 10^{exponent}$ for some power of 10 greater than 1. Consequently, if we put
\[
auxpoly(repunit) = 9polyfun\left( \frac{repunit-1}{9} \right) + 1,
\]
then $polyfun$ takes repunits to repunits if and only if $auxpoly$ takes powers of 10
greater than 1 to powers of 10 greater than 1. We will show that the only
such functions $auxpoly$ are those of the form $auxpoly(repunit) = 10^{shiftcof} repunit^{degreet}$ for $degreet \geq 0$,
$shiftcof \geq 1-degreet$ (all of which clearly work),
which will mean that the desired polynomials $polyfun$ are those of the form
\[
polyfun(repunit) = \frac{1}{9}(10^{shiftcof} (9repunit+1)^{degreet} - 1)
\]
for the same $shiftcof,degreet$.

It is convenient to allow ``powers of 10'' to be of the form
$10^{indexer}$ for any integer $indexer$. With this convention, it suffices to check that
the polynomials $auxpoly$ taking powers of 10 greater than 1 to powers of 10
are of the form $10^{shiftcof} repunit^{degreet}$ for any integers $shiftcof,degreet$ with $degreet \geq 0$.

\textbf{First solution:}
Suppose that the leading term of $auxpoly(variable)$ is $leadcoef variable^{degreet}$, and note that
$leadcoef>0$. As $variable \to \infty$, we have $auxpoly(variable)/variable^{degreet} \to leadcoef$; however, for $variable$
a power of 10 greater than 1, $auxpoly(variable)/variable^{degreet}$ is a power of 10.
The set of powers of 10 has no positive limit point, so $auxpoly(variable)/variable^{degreet}$
must be equal to $leadcoef$ for $variable = 10^{indexer}$ with $indexer$ sufficiently large,
and we must have $leadcoef = 10^{shiftcof}$ for some $shiftcof$.
The polynomial $auxpoly(variable) - 10^{shiftcof} variable^{degreet}$ has infinitely many roots, so
must be identically zero.

\textbf{Second solution:}
We proceed by induction on $degreet = \deg(auxpoly)$.
If $degreet=0$, we have $auxpoly(repunit) = 10^{shiftcof}$ for some $shiftcof$. Otherwise,
$auxpoly$ has rational coefficients by Lagrange's interpolation formula (this
applies to any polynomial of degree $degreet$ taking at least $degreet+1$
different rational numbers to rational numbers), so $auxpoly(0) = constant$ is rational.
Moreover, $auxpoly$ takes each value only finitely many times, so the sequence
$auxpoly(10^0), auxpoly(10^1), \dots$ includes arbitrarily large powers of 10.
Suppose that $constant \neq 0$; then we can choose a positive integer $helperh$ such that
the numerator of $constant$ is not divisible by $10^{helperh}$.
But for $shiftcof$ large enough, $auxpoly(10^{shiftcof}) - constant$ has numerator divisible by
$10^{counterb}$ for some $counterb>helperh$, contradiction.

Consequently, $constant=0$, and we may apply the induction hypothesis to $auxpoly(repunit)/repunit$
to deduce the claim.

\textbf{Remark:} The second solution amounts to the fact that $auxpoly$, being
a polynomial with rational coefficients, is continuous for the $2$-adic
and $5$-adic topologies on $\mathbb{ratfield}$. By contrast, the first solution
uses the ``$\infty$-adic'' topology, i.e., the usual real topology.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
A \emph{repunit} is a positive integer whose digits in base 10 are
all ones. Find all polynomials $umbrella$ with real coefficients such that if
$backpack$ is a repunit, then so is $umbrella(backpack)$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Note that $backpack$ is a repunit if and only if $9backpack+1 = 10^{playwright}$ for some power of
10 greater than 1. Consequently, if we put
\[
sailboat(backpack) = 9umbrella\left( \frac{backpack-1}{9} \right) + 1,
\]
then $umbrella$ takes repunits to repunits if and only if $sailboat$ takes powers of 10
greater than 1 to powers of 10 greater than 1. We will show that the only
such functions $sailboat$ are those of the form $sailboat(backpack) = 10^{raincloud} backpack^{marigolds}$ for $marigolds \geq 0$,
$raincloud \geq 1-marigolds$ (all of which clearly work),
which will mean that the desired polynomials $umbrella$ are those of the form
\[
umbrella(backpack) = \frac{1}{9}(10^{raincloud} (9backpack+1)^{marigolds} - 1)
\]
for the same $raincloud,marigolds$.

It is convenient to allow ``powers of 10'' to be of the form
$10^{doorstep}$ for any integer $doorstep$. With this convention, it suffices to check that
the polynomials $sailboat$ taking powers of 10 greater than 1 to powers of 10
are of the form $10^{raincloud} backpack^{marigolds}$ for any integers $raincloud,marigolds$ with $marigolds \geq 0$.

\textbf{First solution:}
Suppose that the leading term of $sailboat(lanterns)$ is $sunflower lanterns^{marigolds}$, and note that
$sunflower>0$. As $lanterns \to \infty$, we have $sailboat(lanterns)/lanterns^{marigolds} \to sunflower$; however, for $lanterns$
a power of 10 greater than 1, $sailboat(lanterns)/lanterns^{marigolds}$ is a power of 10.
The set of powers of 10 has no positive limit point, so $sailboat(lanterns)/lanterns^{marigolds}$
must be equal to $sunflower$ for $lanterns = 10^{doorstep}$ with $doorstep$ sufficiently large,
and we must have $sunflower = 10^{raincloud}$ for some $raincloud$.
The polynomial $sailboat(lanterns) - 10^{raincloud} lanterns^{marigolds}$ has infinitely many roots, so
must be identically zero.

\textbf{Second solution:}
We proceed by induction on $marigolds = \deg(sailboat)$.
If $marigolds=0$, we have $sailboat(backpack) = 10^{raincloud}$ for some $raincloud$. Otherwise,
$sailboat$ has rational coefficients by Lagrange's interpolation formula (this
applies to any polynomial of degree $marigolds$ taking at least $marigolds+1$
different rational numbers to rational numbers), so $sailboat(0) = sandstone$ is rational.
Moreover, $sailboat$ takes each value only finitely many times, so the sequence
$sailboat(10^0), sailboat(10^1), \dots$ includes arbitrarily large powers of 10.
Suppose that $sandstone \neq 0$; then we can choose a positive integer $pancakes$ such that
the numerator of $sandstone$ is not divisible by $10^{pancakes}$.
But for $raincloud$ large enough, $sailboat(10^{raincloud}) - sandstone$ has numerator divisible by
$10^{necklace}$ for some $necklace>pancakes$, contradiction.

Consequently, $sandstone=0$, and we may apply the induction hypothesis to $sailboat(backpack)/backpack$
to deduce the claim.

\textbf{Remark:} The second solution amounts to the fact that $sailboat$, being
a polynomial with rational coefficients, is continuous for the $2$-adic
and $5$-adic topologies on $\mathbb{lemonade}$. By contrast, the first solution
uses the ``$\infty$-adic'' topology, i.e., the usual real topology.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
A \emph{repunit} is a positive integer whose digits in base 10 are
all ones. Find all polynomials $constantfunction$ with real coefficients such that if
$nonrepunit$ is a repunit, then so is $constantfunction(nonrepunit)$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Note that $nonrepunit$ is a repunit if and only if $9nonrepunit+1 = 10^{fractionalindex}$ for some power of
10 greater than 1. Consequently, if we put
\[
randommapping(nonrepunit) = 9constantfunction\left( \frac{nonrepunit-1}{9} \right) + 1,
\]
then $constantfunction$ takes repunits to repunits if and only if $randommapping$ takes powers of 10
greater than 1 to powers of 10 greater than 1. We will show that the only
such functions $randommapping$ are those of the form $randommapping(nonrepunit) = 10^{mantissavalue} nonrepunit^{nodegree}$ for $nodegree \geq 0$,
$mantissavalue \geq 1-nodegree$ (all of which clearly work),
which will mean that the desired polynomials $constantfunction$ are those of the form
\[
constantfunction(nonrepunit) = \frac{1}{9}(10^{mantissavalue} (9nonrepunit+1)^{nodegree} - 1)
\]
for the same $mantissavalue,nodegree$.

It is convenient to allow ``powers of 10'' to be of the form
$10^{nonintegerindex}$ for any integer $nonintegerindex$. With this convention, it suffices to check that
the polynomials $randommapping$ taking powers of 10 greater than 1 to powers of 10
are of the form $10^{mantissavalue} nonrepunit^{nodegree}$ for any integers $mantissavalue,nodegree$ with $nodegree \geq 0$.

\textbf{First solution:}
Suppose that the leading term of $randommapping(constantvalue)$ is $trailingcoef constantvalue^{nodegree}$, and note that
$trailingcoef>0$. As $constantvalue \to \infty$, we have $randommapping(constantvalue)/constantvalue^{nodegree} \to trailingcoef$; however, for $constantvalue$
a power of 10 greater than 1, $randommapping(constantvalue)/constantvalue^{nodegree}$ is a power of 10.
The set of powers of 10 has no positive limit point, so $randommapping(constantvalue)/constantvalue^{nodegree}$
must be equal to $trailingcoef$ for $constantvalue = 10^{nonintegerindex}$ with $nonintegerindex$ sufficiently large,
and we must have $trailingcoef = 10^{mantissavalue}$ for some $mantissavalue$.
The polynomial $randommapping(constantvalue) - 10^{mantissavalue} constantvalue^{nodegree}$ has infinitely many roots, so
must be identically zero.

\textbf{Second solution:}
We proceed by induction on $nodegree = \deg(randommapping)$.
If $nodegree=0$, we have $randommapping(nonrepunit) = 10^{mantissavalue}$ for some $mantissavalue$. Otherwise,
$randommapping$ has rational coefficients by Lagrange's interpolation formula (this
applies to any polynomial of degree $nodegree$ taking at least $nodegree+1$
different rational numbers to rational numbers), so $randommapping(0) = irrationalvalue$ is rational.
Moreover, $randommapping$ takes each value only finitely many times, so the sequence
$randommapping(10^0), randommapping(10^1), \dots$ includes arbitrarily large powers of 10.
Suppose that $irrationalvalue \neq 0$; then we can choose a positive integer $nonpositiveindex$ such that
the numerator of $irrationalvalue$ is not divisible by $10^{nonpositiveindex}$.
But for $mantissavalue$ large enough, $randommapping(10^{mantissavalue}) - irrationalvalue$ has numerator divisible by
$10^{tinyindex}$ for some $tinyindex>nonpositiveindex$, contradiction.

Consequently, $irrationalvalue=0$, and we may apply the induction hypothesis to $randommapping(nonrepunit)/nonrepunit$
to deduce the claim.

\textbf{Remark:} The second solution amounts to the fact that $randommapping$, being
a polynomial with rational coefficients, is continuous for the $2$-adic
and $5$-adic topologies on $\mathbb{irrationals}$. By contrast, the first solution
uses the ``$\infty$-adic'' topology, i.e., the usual real topology.

garbled_string.question: A \emph{repunit} is a positive integer whose digits in base 10 are all ones. Find all polynomials $lktbqmds$ with real coefficients such that if $qzxwvtnp$ is a repunit, then so is $lktbqmds(qzxwvtnp)$.
garbled_string.solution:
Note that $qzxwvtnp$ is a repunit if and only if $9qzxwvtnp+1 = 10^{rntcvzqb}$ for some power of 10 greater than 1. Consequently, if we put
\[
wvcfzhgo(qzxwvtnp) = 9lktbqmds\left( \frac{qzxwvtnp-1}{9} \right) + 1,
\]
then $lktbqmds$ takes repunits to repunits if and only if $wvcfzhgo$ takes powers of 10 greater than 1 to powers of 10 greater than 1. We will show that the only such functions $wvcfzhgo$ are those of the form $wvcfzhgo(qzxwvtnp) = 10^{pgmlvhza} qzxwvtnp^{xzbrkphs}$ for $xzbrkphs \geq 0$, $pgmlvhza \geq 1 - xzbrkphs$ (all of which clearly work), which will mean that the desired polynomials $lktbqmds$ are those of the form
\[
lktbqmds(qzxwvtnp) = \frac{1}{9}(10^{pgmlvhza} (9qzxwvtnp+1)^{xzbrkphs} - 1)
\]
for the same $pgmlvhza,xzbrkphs$.

It is convenient to allow ``powers of 10'' to be of the form $10^{dpslqjkr}$ for any integer $dpslqjkr$. With this convention, it suffices to check that the polynomials $wvcfzhgo$ taking powers of 10 greater than 1 to powers of 10 are of the form $10^{pgmlvhza} qzxwvtnp^{xzbrkphs}$ for any integers $pgmlvhza,xzbrkphs$ with $xzbrkphs \geq 0$.

\textbf{First solution:}
Suppose that the leading term of $wvcfzhgo(hjgrksla)$ is $srmpdqlt hjgrksla^{xzbrkphs}$, and note that $srmpdqlt>0$. As $hjgrksla \to \infty$, we have $wvcfzhgo(hjgrksla)/hjgrksla^{xzbrkphs} \to srmpdqlt$; however, for $hjgrksla$ a power of 10 greater than 1, $wvcfzhgo(hjgrksla)/hjgrksla^{xzbrkphs}$ is a power of 10. The set of powers of 10 has no positive limit point, so $wvcfzhgo(hjgrksla)/hjgrksla^{xzbrkphs}$ must be equal to $srmpdqlt$ for $hjgrksla = 10^{dpslqjkr}$ with $dpslqjkr$ sufficiently large, and we must have $srmpdqlt = 10^{pgmlvhza}$ for some $pgmlvhza$. The polynomial $wvcfzhgo(hjgrksla) - 10^{pgmlvhza} hjgrksla^{xzbrkphs}$ has infinitely many roots, so must be identically zero.

\textbf{Second solution:}
We proceed by induction on $xzbrkphs = \deg(wvcfzhgo)$. If $xzbrkphs = 0$, we have $wvcfzhgo(qzxwvtnp) = 10^{pgmlvhza}$ for some $pgmlvhza$. Otherwise, $wvcfzhgo$ has rational coefficients by Lagrange's interpolation formula (this applies to any polynomial of degree $xzbrkphs$ taking at least $xzbrkphs+1$ different rational numbers to rational numbers), so $wvcfzhgo(0) = vchlqtsm$ is rational. Moreover, $wvcfzhgo$ takes each value only finitely many times, so the sequence $wvcfzhgo(10^0), wvcfzhgo(10^1), \dots$ includes arbitrarily large powers of 10.

Suppose that $vchlqtsm \neq 0$; then we can choose a positive integer $yzjwndvo$ such that the numerator of $vchlqtsm$ is not divisible by $10^{yzjwndvo}$. But for $pgmlvhza$ large enough, $wvcfzhgo(10^{pgmlvhza}) - vchlqtsm$ has numerator divisible by $10^{fkpslgjh}$ for some $fkpslgjh > yzjwndvo$, contradiction.

Consequently, $vchlqtsm = 0$, and we may apply the induction hypothesis to $wvcfzhgo(qzxwvtnp)/qzxwvtnp$ to deduce the claim.

\textbf{Remark:} The second solution amounts to the fact that $wvcfzhgo$, being a polynomial with rational coefficients, is continuous for the $2$-adic and $5$-adic topologies on $\mathbb{lsphqjdm}$. By contrast, the first solution uses the ``$\infty$-adic'' topology, i.e., the usual real topology.

kernel_variant.question:
Call a positive integer an \emph{octal repunit} if its representation in base $8$ consists entirely of the digit $1$.  (Thus the first few octal repunits are $1,\;9,\;73,\;585,\dots$ in ordinary decimal notation.)

Determine all polynomials $f\!:\!\mathbb Q\to\mathbb Q$ such that whenever $n$ is an octal repunit, $f(n)$ is again an octal repunit.
kernel_variant.solution:
Write b=8.  A positive integer n is an octal repunit with m\geq 1 digits exactly when

  7n+1 = b^m.

If f \in  Q[x] carries every octal repunit to an octal repunit, define

  g(x) = 7\cdot f((x-1)/7) + 1.

Then for every m \geq  1,

  x = b^m \Rightarrow  g(x) = b^k  for some k \geq  1,

so g maps the infinite geometric sequence {b^m} to itself.  Let deg g = d and write

  g(x) = a x^d + (lower terms).

Then for x = b^m,

  g(b^m)/b^{m d} = a + o(1)

must lie in the discrete set {\ldots , b^{-1}, 1, b, b^2, \ldots }, so for large m it stabilizes at a = b^c with c \in  Z.  Hence

  g(x) - b^c x^d

has infinitely many roots b^m and is identically zero, giving

  g(x) = b^c x^d.

Substitute back:

  7\cdot f((x-1)/7) + 1 = b^c x^d,

so with x = 7x' + 1,

  f(x') = (1/7)(b^c (7x' + 1)^d - 1).

The requirement that g(b^m) = b^{c + m d} have exponent \geq  1 for all m \geq  1 forces

  c + d \geq  1,

i.e.

  d \in  Z_{\geq 0},   c \in  Z,   c \geq  1 - d.

Conversely, any such c,d make f \in  Q[x], and for n = (b^m - 1)/7 one checks 7f(n)+1 = b^{c+md}, so f(n) is an octal repunit.  Hence the complete solution is

  f(x) = (1/7)(8^c (7x + 1)^d - 1),

where d \geq  0, c \geq  1 - d, and c,d \in  Z.

core_steps: Repunit Ōćö linear transformation to a power of the base:  (baseŌłÆ1)n+1 = base^m
Introduce g so that g maps (base-)powers to (base-)powers
Compare g(x)/x^d on an unbounded geometric sequence; lack of accumulation ŌćÆ ratio stabilises to base^c
Infinite coincidence points force g(x) ŌēĪ base^c┬Ęx^d
Undo the linear change of variables to obtain f

slot1.description: Choice of numeral base in which the repunit is expressed

slot2.description: Factor (baseŌłÆ1) appearing in the identity (baseŌłÆ1)n+1 = base^m and in the definition of g

slot3.description: Geometric set that g is required to permute (powers of the chosen base)

slot4.description: Lower bound on the exponents considered (e.g. ŌĆśgreater than 1ŌĆÖ); any finite cutoff works

slot5.description: Allowed coefficient field for the polynomial (needs to sit inside ŌäØ so limits make sense)
